coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoclusters assisted construction of a ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for detection of Gram-negative bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Li
Chen, Qingmei
Jia, Li
coredata.dc:description: A core-satellite nanocomposite was prepared by encapsulating the photostable blue carbon dots (BCDs) in the core of silica as the reference signal readout, and the target-sensitive gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) covalently linked to the surface of silica as the respond signal readout. The nanocomposite (BCD@SiO2@AuNC) was used as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to realize the selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria. The detection principle was based on the quenching of Cu2+ toward AuNCs and the reduction of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+. The sensor exhibited good selectivity toward Gram-negative bacteria owing to the copper-homeostasis mechanism possessed by the bacteria. The sensor demonstrated linear response to the logarithm concentration of Gram-negative bacteria with determination coefficients higher than 0.912. The feasibility of the sensor was verified by analysis of Gram-negative bacteria in eggshell, swimming pool water, as well as Chinese cabbage samples with recoveries ranging from 93.9% to 109%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Copper ion
Bacteria
Determination
Copper-homeostasis mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Encapsulation of BCDs in amino-functionalized silica spheres
Detection of bacteria in eggshell, swimming pool water, and Chinese cabbage samples
Mechanism of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC for detection of Gram-negative bacteria
BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cu2+ detection
BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Gram-negative bacteria detection
Analysis of eggshell, swimming pool water and Chinese cabbage samples
Comparison of the biosensor with other sensors targeting at detection of Gram-negative bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detection of Gram-negative bacteria.
TEM images of (A) BCDs, (B) BSA-AuNCs, (C) BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC. (D) Fluorescence spectra of BCDs, BSA-AuNCs and BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC.
Fluorescence spectra of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC (a), Cu2+ + BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC (b), Cu2+ + E. coli + BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC (c) at λex = 370 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC at λex = 370 nm on exposure to various concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 7.0 × 10-6 M. (B) Plot of F650/F450 versus the concentration of Cu2+ in carbonate solution (10 mM, pH 11.0). The inset is the photos of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC (a), and Cu2+ (7.0 × 10−6 M) + BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC (b) under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp.
Fluorescence spectra (λex = 370 nm) of the BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC-based biosensor by addition of different bacteria (1.0 × 106 cfu mL−1).

(A) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 370 nm) of the BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC-based biosensor with various concentrations of E. coli in eggshell. (B) Linear calibration curve of the increase in the fluorescence intensity ratio [Δ (F650/F450)] versus the logarithm concentration of E. coli in eggshell.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoclusters assisted construction of a ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for detection of Gram-negative bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science & Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Spectral Analysis and Functional Probes, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A core-satellite nanocomposite was prepared by encapsulating the photostable blue carbon dots (BCDs) in the core of silica as the reference signal readout, and the target-sensitive gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) covalently linked to the surface of silica as the respond signal readout. The nanocomposite (BCD@SiO2@AuNC) was used as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to realize the selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria. The detection principle was based on the quenching of Cu2+ toward AuNCs and the reduction of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+. The sensor exhibited good selectivity toward Gram-negative bacteria owing to the copper-homeostasis mechanism possessed by the bacteria. The sensor demonstrated linear response to the logarithm concentration of Gram-negative bacteria with determination coefficients higher than 0.912. The feasibility of the sensor was verified by analysis of Gram-negative bacteria in eggshell, swimming pool water, as well as Chinese cabbage samples with recoveries ranging from 93.9% to 109%.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic bacterial infections and associated foodborne illnesses have emerged as a giant burden on public health worldwide (Rao, Tian, & Chen, 2020). Therefore, effective detection of pathogens in contaminated water and food is an essential prerequisite for the timely prevention and control of foodborne outbreaks. The culture and colony counting method is the most reliable assay for bacteria detection (Kim, Lee, Lee, & Cho, 2012). However, it is a lengthy process, typically over 24 h from sampling to results, which may delay the timely treatment of patients and control of an epidemic. Immunologic and polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based molecular methods can effectively reduce detection time, but they require specialized equipment and trained personnel (Ding, Suo, Zhang, Liu, Ye, Chen, et al., 2017). And oftentimes these assays cannot avert time-consuming culture step in order to detect low concentrations of bacteria.

Given the limitations of these assays, numerous efforts have been devoted to the development of alternative approaches to address these issues. Among these approaches, optical biosensors offer the possible answers to these issues since they are cost-effective, user-friendly, robust, rapid, as well as portable (Soler, Estevez, Cardenosa-Rubio, Astua, & Lechuga, 2020). Especially, fluorescent biosensors are highly attractive due to their unique advantages of high sensitivity, convenience, and inexpensive equipment required (Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Nayak, Mehta, Kim, & Deep, 2017).

Fluorophores are essential in fluorescent sensors, and many prominent nanomaterials, such as gold nanoparticles, quantum dots, silica nanobeads, as well as up-conversion nanoparticles, have been applied for the detection of pathogens on account of their superior photostability and facile synthesis (Baker & Baker, 2010; Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Nayak, Mehta, Kim, & Deep, 2017; Lu & Chen, 2012). Most of the fluorescent nanoparticles-based biosensors are single-emission. Single-emission fluorescent biosensors are more easily subjected to interferences from a variety of analyte-independent factors, such as instrumental parameters, microenvironment around the bacteria, and local concentration variation of probes (Huang, Song, Yung, Huang, Xiong, & Chen, 2018; Zong, Ai, Zhang, Li, & Lu, 2011).

To overcome the difficulties faced by single-emission fluorescent biosensors, the use of ratiometric fluorescent biosensors is the most effective strategy (Gui, Jin, Bu, Fu, Wang, & Liu, 2019). Ratiometric fluorescent biosensors rely on the simultaneous measurement of two fluorescence signals at different wavelengths. The ratio of intensities at two wavelengths directly correlates with the concentration of the target analyte. As a result, the biosensors possess built-in self-calibration systems for the correction of various target-independent factors, which greatly increase their sensitivity and improve quantification. Some ratiometric fluorescent biosensors have been constructed for the detection of pathogens (Long, Miao, Li, Deng, Qiao, Liu, et al., 2019; Long, Qiao, Miao, & Xu, 2019; Na, Zhang, Liu, Ma, Han, Wang, et al., 2020; Shen, Wu, Zhang, Ling, Zheng, Zhang, et al., 2020; Svechkarev, Sadykov, Bayles, & Mohs, 2018). Regretfully, most of the ratiometric fluorescent probes are organic dyes, and they suffer from photobleaching. Fluorescent nanomaterials are seldom applied for the construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensors for pathogens detection. Na et al. synthesized europium-doped silicon nanoparticles with two emission bands, which were used as a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of the biomarker (dipicolinic acid) of anthrax spores (Na, et al., 2020). Carbon dots and aptamer/vancomycin modified CdTe quantum dots were used to assemble a ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for tracking of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Shen, et al., 2020). So far, few reports have been disclosed that the assembly of a core-satellite nanostructure by integrating two different fluorescent nanomaterials, which was used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to realize the detection of pathogens.

Herein, a core-satellite nanostructure was prepared through the silica coating strategy and used as a ratiometric sensor to realize the detection of Gram-negative bacteria. The sensor employed the photostable blue carbon dots (BCDs) embedded in the core of the silica sphere as the reference signal readout, and the target-sensitive gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) covalently linked on the surface of the silica sphere as the respond signal readout. BCDs were ideal to be the reference signal readouts owing to their unique advantages including high quantum yield, photostability, cost-efficiency, as well as eco-friend (Baker & Baker, 2010). AuNCs were chosen as the response signal readout because they exhibit large Stokes shift under the same excitation wavelength as that of BCDs. Also, they possess high quantum yield, good stability, and well biocompatibility (Lu & Chen, 2012). More importantly, bovine serum albumin stabilized AuNCs (BSA-AuNCs) can be quenched by copper ion (Cu2+) due to the coordinate interaction between Cu2+ and the amino acids in BSA, which induces the energy loss of the excited electron of BSA-AuNCs (Durgadas, Sharma, & Sreenivasan, 2011). Interestingly, cuprous ion (Cu+) was unable to quench the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs (Li, Kumari, Makabenta, Gupta, & Rotello, 2019).

More interestingly, Gram-negative bacteria can tolerate copper-rich environments by the copper-binding system to bind Cu2+ and redox enzyme cascades to reduce Cu2+ to Cu+, which is known as the copper homeostasis mechanism (Hodgkinson & Petris, 2012; Rensing & Grass, 2003; Solioz, 2018). Inspired by the interesting function of Gram-negative bacteria, the core-satellite nanostructure (BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC) was used to engineer a ratiometric fluorescent biosensor to realize the selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs can be dramatically quenched by Cu2+ via the formation of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC-Cu2+ ensemble. In contrast, the fluorescence of BCDs was not affected because of the protection of the silica shell. In the presence of Gram-negative bacteria, Cu2+ can be reduced to Cu+ through the copper-binding system and redox pathways, thus leading to the alleviation of the fluorescence quenching of BSA-AuNCs. Therefore, evaluation of the reduction ability of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+ based on BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC can realize the detection and quantification of Gram-negative bacteria. The feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescent biosensor was investigated in authentic specimens including eggshell, swimming pool water, as well as Chinese cabbage samples.

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade or better. Citric acid (99.5%), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), and anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), were supplied by Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Tetrachloroauric (III) acid hydrate (HAuCl4⋅4H2O, Au ≥ 47.8%) was acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). BSA (≥98.0%) was acquired from Shanghai Bio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonia hydroxide (NH4OH, 25%), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Ethylenediamine and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were supplied by Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) were provided by J&K Chemical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

Ultra-violet–Visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired on a K5600 micro-spectrophotometer (Beijing Kaiao Technology Development Company, Beijing, China). Fluorescence spectra were acquired on a F-380A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Sci. & Tech. Development Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on an iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA).

BCDs were prepared according to a previously reported method (Zhu, Meng, Wang, Zhang, Song, Jin, et al., 2013) with some modifications. Briefly, citric acid (840.0 mg) and ethylenediamine (1.2 mL) were dissolved in deionized water (20.0 mL). Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After that, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The dark yellow product was dialyzed against water for 6 h by a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) to remove the unreacted impurities. The resultant BCDs solution was stored at 4 °C prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation of BCDs in amino-functionalized silica spheres
ce:section.ce:para: The encapsulation of BCDs in amino-functionalized silica spheres was performed in a two-step process. First, BCDs were encapsulated in silica spheres by a Stöber method (Stöber, Fink, & Bohn, 1968; Yang, Li, Sun, Ming, Li, Wang, et al., 2009). BCDs (2.5 mL, 24.4 mg/mL) was added to ethanol (12.5 mL), followed by addition of ammonia hydroxide (0.1 mL, 25%). Then the solution was transferred to a flask and vigorously stirred for 5 min. After that, TEOS (50.0 μL) was added to the above solution and the mixture was kept stirring at ambient temperature for 3 h. Next, APTES (50.0 μL) was added to the mixture and stirred overnight. Subsequently, the products (BCD@SiO2) were collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and deionized water three times. Finally, the products were redispersed in deionized water (10.0 mL) for further use.

BSA-AuNCs were synthesized based on a previously reported method with some modifications (Chen, Gao, & Jia, 2021; Xie, Zheng, & Ying, 2009). Briefly, BSA (750.0 mg) was first dissolved in deionized water (25.0 mL), followed by the addition of HAuCl4 solution (6.0 mL, 25 mM) under vigorous stirring. Two minutes later, the NaOH solution (1.5 mL, 1 M) was introduced and the mixture was stirred at 37 °C for 24 h. After that, the formed deep brown product (BSA-AuNCs) was dialyzed against water for 12 h by a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO). The resultant BSA-AuNCs solution was stored at 4 °C prior to use. The concentration of BSA-AuNCs was determined by a freeze-drying and weighting method.

The BSA-AuNCs solution (2.0 mL, 5.3 mg/mL) was added to a flask containing NHS/EDC aqueous solution (4.0 mL, each 2 mg/mL). Then, the mixture was stirred for 0.5 h to activate the carboxyl group of BSA-AuNCs. Following that, the BCD@SiO2 solution (0.3 mL) was added to the mixture and was kept stirring overnight. The formed BCD@SiO2@AuNCs were collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and ultrapure water three times. Finally, the product was redispersed in deionized water (6.0 mL).

The Gram-negative bacteria, including Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli) (GW 1.0202), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) (ATCC 15442), Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium) (ATCC14028), and the Gram-positive bacteria, including Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (CMCC 26003) and Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) (CMCC 54007), were used in the experiments. The Gram-negative bacteria strains were cultured in Luria-Bertani broth (10 mg mL−1 tryptone, 5 mg mL−1 yeast extract, 10 mg mL−1 NaCl, pH 7.0) under aerobic conditions at 37 °C for 10 h with shaking at 200 rpm on a ZHWY-103B shaker (Shanghai Zhicheng Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The Gram-positive bacteria strains were cultured in the broth (10 mg mL−1 tryptone, 3 mg mL−1 beef extract, 5 mg mL−1 NaCl, pH 7.0) under aerobic conditions at 37 °C for 10 h with shaking at 200 rpm. After that, the bacteria cells were harvested through centrifugation. A required concentration of bacteria was adjusted by measuring the optical density at 600 nm (OD600).

The BCD@SiO2@AuNCs solution (20 μL) was added to the carbonate solution (1.0 mL, 10 mM, pH 11.0) containing different concentrations of Cu2+ ranging from 0 to 9.0 × 10−6 M. The mixture was thoroughly mixed for 3 min and taken for fluorescent measurements. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the emission wavelength range from 400 nm to 700 nm with the excitation wavelength at 370 nm, slits at 10.0, and voltage at 700 V.

The ratiometric fluorescent determination of bacteria was carried out according to the following procedure, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . Typically, different concentrations of bacteria were added to the carbonate solution (1.0 mL, 10 mM, pH 11.0) containing 7.0 × 10−6 M Cu2+, followed by incubation at ambient temperature for 1.5 h with shaking at 200 rpm. After that, the BCD@SiO2@AuNCs solution (20.0 μL) was added to the suspension and mixed thoroughly. Three minutes later, the fluorescence spectra were recorded in the emission wavelength range from 400 nm to 700 nm with the excitation wavelength at 370 nm, slits at 10.0, and voltage at 700 V. All experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of bacteria in eggshell, swimming pool water, and Chinese cabbage samples
ce:section.ce:para: The egg samples were purchased from a local market (Guangzhou, China). Firstly, the eggs were washed several times with deionized water. Then the samples were placed in a sterile clean room and soaked in 75% (v/v) ethanol for 0.5 h at ambient temperature. After drying by air, the eggshell (100.0 mg) was ground and mixed with the carbonate solution (10.0 mL, 10 mM, pH 11.0). The mixture was incubated in a rotary mixer (Crystal Technology & Industries, Inc., Dallas, USA) for 12 h. Subsequently, the supernatant of the eggshell mixture was collected as the eggshell soaking solution samples.
The swimming pool water samples were collected from a residential area (Guangzhou, China). Then, the swimming pool water samples were prepared as the solution containing carbonate (10 mM, pH 11.0).

The Chinese cabbage samples were purchased from a local market (Guangzhou, China). Firstly, the Chinese cabbage was washed several times with deionized water. Then the samples were placed in a sterile clean room and dried by air. After that, the Chinese cabbage (400.0 mg) was cut into about 3 × 3 mm and mixed with the carbonate solution (10.0 mL, 10 mM, pH 11.0). The mixture was incubated in a rotary mixer (Crystal Technology & Industries, Inc., Dallas, USA) for 15 min. Subsequently, the supernatant of the Chinese cabbage mixture was collected as the Chinese cabbage solution samples.
The spiked samples were prepared by adding various concentrations of bacteria to the eggshell soaking solution, swimming pool water, and Chinese cabbage solution samples.

The BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC nanocomposites were prepared by a three-step process. First, BCDs were embedded in a silica sphere through a modified Stöber method. Second, the surface of silica spheres was modified with APTES to form amino-functionalized silica spheres (BCD@SiO2). Third, BSA-AuNCs were treated with EDC and NHS to activate the carboxyl group of BSA. Then, BSA-AuNCs were covalently linked to the surface of silica spheres. When we synthesized the BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC nanostructure, the amount of BCDs was first fixed. Then, the percentage of SiO2 shell was optimized. The thickness of the silica shell should prevent the fluorescence of BCDs in the core from being quenched by Cu2+ (Hu, Yu, Li, Wang, Yang, Zhao, et al., 2014). However, the dispersibility of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC will be affected if the silica shell is too thick. Next, the percentage of BSA-AuNCs was optimized based on the reduction ability of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+. The amount of BSA-AuNCs was adjusted to ensure that their fluorescence was fully quenched by 7.0 × 10−6 M Cu2+. Based on the aforementioned optimization experiments, the mass ratio of BCDs to BSA-AuNCs was about 1:5.8. The final products (BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC) redispersed in water emitted pink fluorescence owning to the dual-emission of BCDs and BSA-AuNCs under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp. And the biosensor exhibited the highest efficiency for the detection of Gram-negative bacteria.

The particle sizes and morphology information of BCDs, BSA-AuNCs, and BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC were characterized by TEM images. As shown in Fig. 2 A and 2B, BCDs and BSA-AuNCs disperse uniformly, and the average particle sizes of BCDs and BSA-AuNCs are approximately 2.7 nm and 1.8 nm, respectively. Fig. 2C demonstrates that the particle size of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC is about 248 nm, and the surface of the nanocomposites is relatively rough, suggesting that BSA-AuNCs are successfully modified on the surface of BCD@SiO2.

The functional groups of BCDs, BSA-AuNCs, and BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC were characterized by FT-IR spectrometry, as depicted in Fig. S1. The spectrum of BCDs shows that BCDs contain –OH, –NH, and CO groups, as evidenced by the peaks at 3445 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1. In the spectrum of BSA-AuNCs, the peak at 3445 cm−1 results from –OH, and –NH groups. The peaks at 1634 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 are respectively attributed to the CO and CN stretching vibrations. The characteristic peaks of BCD@SiO2 and BSA-AuNCs appear in the spectrum of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC. It is noteworthy that the peak located at 1045 cm−1 becomes broadened owing to the existence of Si-O-Si stretching vibration from the silica shell. The results attest to the modification of BSA-AuNCs on the surface of BCD@SiO2.

The luminescent properties of BCDs, BSA-AuNCs, and BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2D). Free BCDs and BSA-AuNCs respectively display the emission peaks at 450 nm and 658 nm under the excitation at 370 nm. As we expected, BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC exhibits dual emission peaks at 450 nm and 650 nm under λex = 370 nm. It should be noted that the emission shift of BSA-AuNCs in the nanocomposites occurs, compared to the free BSA-AuNCs, suggesting that the photophysical property of BSA-AuNCs in the nanocomposites happens to change. During the process for activation and covalently linking of BSA-AuNCs, the change of BSA conformation results in the dissociation of some of the gold atoms, thus leading to the blue shift of fluorescence emission of BSA-AuNCs (Shichibu, Negishi, Tsunoyama, Kanehara, Teranishi, & Tsukuda, 2007; Varnavski, Ramakrishna, Kim, Lee, & Goodson, 2010). The phenomenon further evidences that BSA-AuNCs were successfully attached to the surface of BCD@SiO2.

Furthermore, the stability of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC was examined. As depicted in Fig. S2, the fluorescence ratio (F650/F450) of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC shows negligible change under illumination by UV light (370 nm) for 1 h, indicating the good photostability of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC for detection of Gram-negative bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs can be quenched by Cu2+ instead of Cu+ (Li, Kumari, Makabenta, Gupta, & Rotello, 2019). Meanwhile, Gram-negative bacteria can effectively bind Cu2+ and reduce it to Cu+ based on the copper-homeostasis mechanism (Hodgkinson & Petris, 2012; Rensing & Grass, 2003; Solioz, 2018). To investigate the mechanism of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC for the detection of Gram-negative bacteria, E. coli was chosen as the model. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs can be dramatically quenched by Cu2+ via the formation of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC-Cu2+ ensemble. In contrast, the blue emission of the embedded BCDs remains unchanged because of the protection of the silica shell. In the presence of Gram-negative bacteria, the Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching effect of BSA-AuNCs is supposed to be mitigated because Cu2+ can be reduced to Cu+ through the copper-homeostasis mechanism. In other words, the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs in the mixture of Cu2+ and Gram-negative bacteria should be stronger than that without Gram-negative bacteria, as evidenced in Fig. 3. Therefore, the detection and quantification of Gram-negative bacteria can be realized by evaluation of the reduction ability of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+ based on BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Since the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs can be dramatically quenched by Cu2+, the realization of Cu2+ detection using BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC as a ratiometric fluorescent probe was anticipated. Given the conditions of bacteria detection, the carbonate solution (10 mM, pH 11.0) and incubation time of 3 min were used as the conditions for Cu2+ detection. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs at 650 nm declines with the increase of the concentration of Cu2+ ranging from 1.0 × 10−6 M to 7.0 × 10−6 M, suggesting that BSA-AuNCs are a Cu2+-sensitive respond signal readout. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs encapsulated in the silica shell remains almost unchanged, suggesting the effectiveness of BCDs as an internal reference. The inset of Fig. 4A displays the apparent color change of the BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC solutions from pink to blue in the absence and presence of Cu2+ under the irradiation of UV light at 365 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F650/F450) exhibits a good linear response to the concentration of Cu2+ with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.990, as shown in Fig. 4B. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to 2.5 × 10−7 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F650/F450) reaches a plateau when the concentration of Cu2+ is beyond 7.0 × 10−6 M. Thus, the concentration of Cu2+ (7.0 × 10−6 M) was used for the subsequent condition for bacteria detection.

The selectivity of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC toward Cu2+ was examined. As shown in Fig. S3, BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC are nearly inert to other metal ions, and only Cu2+ triggers a noticeable change in the fluorescence intensity ratio (F650/F450), indicating the excellent selectivity of BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC toward Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Gram-negative bacteria detection
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli was used as a model bacterium to optimize the conditions for bacteria detection. Since the reduction of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+ is a crucial factor to realize the bacteria detection by the constructed ratiometric fluorescent biosensor, the conditions influencing the reduction of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+ were investigated. First, the effect of sample pH on the bacteria detection was studied by keeping the concentration of Cu2+ at 7.0 × 10−6 M and the concentration of E. coli at 1.0 × 106 cfu mL−1. As depicted in Fig. S4, the influence of E. coli on the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs by Cu2+ at pH 11.0 was more pronounced than those at other pH values, suggesting that pH 11.0 is more favorable for the reduction of Cu2+ by E. coli. The cupric reductase NDH-2 and CopA (a Cu+-translocating P-type ATPase) in Gram-negative bacteria is responsive for Cu2+ reduction (Hodgkinson & Petris, 2012; Rensing & Grass, 2003; Solioz, 2018), and they exhibit more activity under an environment of pH 11.0. In other words, at pH 11.0, more Cu2+ can be reduced to Cu+, resulting in the mitigation of the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs by Cu2+. Next, the effect of the incubation time of Cu2+ and E. coli was studied. Fig. S5 shows that the increase in the fluorescence intensity ratio [Δ(F650/F450)] reaches a plateau beyond 1.5 h. Thus, pH 11.0 and incubation time of 1.5 h were used for quantitative analysis of E. coli and other Gram-negative bacteria. Subsequently, the effect of temperature on the biosensor was examined, as shown in Fig. S6. The change of temperature induced a negligible variation of the fluorescence intensity ratio. In other words, the biosensor has no strict requirements on temperature. Considering the practicability and universality of the biosensor, the ambient temperature was employed.

The quantitative analysis results for the Gram-negative bacteria are listed in Table S1. The increase in the fluorescence intensity ratio [Δ(F650/F450)] displays a good linear correlation versus the logarithm concentration of E. coli in the range of 103 to 107 cfu mL−1 with R2 of 0.943. P. aeruginosa and S. typhimurium were used to investigate whether the biosensor was suitable for the detection of other Gram-negative bacteria. Delightfully, the biosensor also demonstrates good linear responses to the logarithm concentrations of the two Gram-negative bacteria with R2 ≥ 0.910, suggesting the feasibility of the biosensor in the detection of Gram-negative bacteria. The LODs were estimated to be 150 cfu mL−1 for E. coli, 112 cfu mL−1 for P. aeruginosa, and 792 cfu mL−1 for S. typhimurium, respectively, based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

To verify the selectivity of the biosensor toward Gram-negative bacteria, 2 Gram-positive bacteria, namely S. aureus, and L. monocytogenes, were employed. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the increases in the fluorescence intensity ratio [Δ (F650/F450)] of Gram-negative bacteria were more than 7.4 times higher than those of Gram-positive bacteria. The result suggests that the BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC-based biosensor was capable to be a selective and efficacious approach for differentiation and detection of Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of eggshell, swimming pool water and Chinese cabbage samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the fluorescent biosensor in authentic specimens was validated by analysis of eggshell, swimming pool water, and Chinese cabbage samples. No bacteria were detected in the three blank samples according to the plate counting method. Thereby, the spiked eggshell, swimming pool water, and Chinese cabbage with different concentrations of three Gram-negative bacteria were analyzed. Fig. 6 and Figs. S7–S14 illustrate that the increase in the fluorescence intensity ratio [Δ (F650/F450)] linearly correlates with the logarithm concentration of each Gram-negative bacterium in the samples ranging from 103 to 107 cfu mL−1, with R2 ≥ 0.912. The recovery experiments were carried out by adding two different concentrations of each Gram-negative bacterium to three samples, as shown in Table S2. The recoveries range from 93.9% to 109% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 5.4%. The results demonstrate that the biosensor is a good alternative for the differentiation and detection of Gram-negative bacteria in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the biosensor with other sensors targeting at detection of Gram-negative bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The biosensor was compared with previously reported sensors targeting at detection of Gram-negative bacteria (Alexandre, Melo, Furtado, Borges, Figueiredo, Biswas, et al., 2018; Duan, Ning, Song, & Deng, 2014; Su & Li, 2004; Wu, Li, Pan, Li, Song, Rong, et al., 2014; Zhang, Wang, Han, Du, Yu, & Zhang, 2018). As shown in Table S3, the biosensor utilized the copper homeostasis mechanism of Gram-negative bacteria to realize the selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria. Other sensors need to prepare antibodies or aptamers to realize the selectivity toward the target bacteria. In addition, the biosensor outperforms most other sensors in response time. BCDs and AuNCs have been reported to be simply mixed to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of cyanide based on the fact that cyanide can quench the fluorescence of AuNCs but not BCDs (Wang, Qiu, Li, Liu, Jiang, Huang, et al., 2019). However, the detection strategy was not suitable for the detection of Gram-negative bacteria since the fluorescence of both BCDs and AuNCs can be quenched by Cu2+. To provide a stable reference signal, it is necessary to introduce a silica shell to prevent the fluorescence of BCDs from quenching by Cu2+. The core-shell-shell structure BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC was successfully used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to realize the detection of Gram-negative bacteria in eggshell, swimming pool water, and Chinese cabbage samples, evidencing the feasibility of the probe in real samples.

A BCD@SiO2@BSA-AuNC-based ratiometric fluorescent biosensor was developed for differentiation and detection of Gram-negative bacteria on the basis of the quenching of Cu2+ toward AuNCs and the reduction of Gram-negative bacteria toward Cu2+. BCDs encapsulated in the silica shell provide the biosensor an effective internal reference. The biosensor exhibits good selectivity toward Gram-negative bacteria owing to the copper-homeostasis mechanism possessed by the bacteria. The feasibility of the biosensor was verified by successful detection of Gram-negative bacteria in eggshell, swimming pool water, and Chinese cabbage samples with recoveries ranging from 93.9% to 109%. The result suggests that utilizing the functions of bacteria and the fluorescent nanomaterials to engineer biosensors is a promising strategy to realize the selective differentiation and detection of Gram-negative bacteria in real samples.
Li Fu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Qingmei Chen: Validation. Li Jia: Validation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675056).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131750.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
